## [00:00 - 03:01]

Hi, this is Don Crowther. Let's talk about the reframe portion of your webinar. So the reframe in a 60 minute webinar, it's about four to eight minutes, 90 minutes, it's five to 10 minutes, though actually this is a fairly flexible section. So if you've got a reframe that truly is changing the way they think about the world, it can actually expand to be a longer content and take some of your content time up because basically what's happening in the reframe is you're teaching content. You're just teaching a content that helps them to understand that they need to be thinking differently about the world. And so this can very much expand to fill that other time, assuming that it is considered by your listeners to be valuable content that will help them to achieve the transformation that are after. So what does it mean to reframe? To reframe is to change the frame of reference through which they view a situation or a problem. So a frame of reference, the filters that someone uses to look at something. And so I like to call it the criteria filters which people have that cause them to think certain ways. When you change that, then people are able to see the world differently than they could before the reframe happened. So what you're actually doing here is you're causing them to think differently than they had before about the problem or and or the potential solutions to that problem. So let's just step back for a moment historically. For many, many years, people believe that ulcers were caused by stomach acid. And so we did everything and I actually did a little bit of this back when I was in high school. I did I had some ulcer kind of issues. And so it's like I had to do all these acids and all these things to fight the acid in my stomach. And then sure enough, a few years later, a scientist came up with the theory that, oh no, it's not acids at all. It's actually a bacteria. And so the solution for ulcers was not to eat bland foods and take all kinds of acids and everything. It was to take high doses of medicine that kill that bacteria. And interesting enough, the way that that reframe happened was that he ended up actually having to give himself those bacteria had to infect himself to prove that that happened. And when he did it and then solved it through taking antibiotics, then all of a sudden it totally reframed the entire market. And we now look at ulcers entirely different than the way we

## [03:01 - 06:04]

did before. That's a reframe. And so as we're thinking differently about the problem or potential solutions, then that opens us up to new approaches to solve that problem. Antibiotics in this particular case, shifting them from thinking of the situation as a challenge into thinking of it as an opportunity. As long as something is a challenge, they're always crippled in their ability to solve that. As soon as you shift that into an opportunity, then they're empowered to solve it. So just some examples of reframes that have occurred. Jeff Walker's sideways sales letter, a great example of how he came in and said, okay, we've been doing these long sales letters for many, many years. And here's the way people look at them. And so here's the way we solve that through use through talking about the marketing in a different way. As soon as that happened, all of a sudden we've got hundreds of millions of dollars of launches that have happened that would not have been possible without the concept, the reframe of the sideways sales letter, Mahatma Gandhi's nonviolent civil civil disobedience and how that's literally changed the world in so many ways. Elon Musk's electrification, so electric cars as well as reusable rockets totally changed the way we looked at things and the solutions that came out of it. So I'm going to make a statement here. If you go out and find your reframe, you've got your webinar. If you can nail that concept of the reframe, your webinar just naturally flows right out of that and you've got yourself a killer webinar. In addition to that, finding that reframe actually starts a story arc that can carry throughout your entire webinar. What's a story arc? It's the start of a story that you then build upon as you continue with the webinar. Now, not all webinars have a story arc that you're able to draw upon, but when you've got a story arc that really works, then the webinar becomes a story which is so much more useful for so many people and more likely to cause them to actually pay attention and attend the entire thing. When you find your reframe, you also are set up as the visionary person they want to follow. Hey, this person saw something I've never been able to see before. And this reframe actually becomes the value of the entire webinar. You don't need to give them a whole bunch more content because their mind is blown by the reframe itself and they're starting

## [06:04 - 07:59]

to see things, seeing that world in that different way. And consequently, they just want to know how to make your reframe happen in their life. Now, when it comes to reframes, the more totally knew the thought, the better, but you don't have to be totally revolutionary to succeed. So it could be as simple as the old way isn't working. And so the challenge of the old way was you did this, and it didn't work. And here's some statistics and here's some examples. Here's cost of failures. This is how you're presenting this reframe. So the old way didn't work. And here's all these this proof that it didn't. And the opportunity is you do this instead and it works and you've got statistics and examples and benefits of success that come out of all that. So this reframe, ideally, as I said, the more revolutionary, the better, but it doesn't have to be totally revolutionary to succeed in talking about a reframe. And then once you've done this comparison in contrast to what it used to be versus what it is now, then you drop into sharing your story. And I have a whole training about that that you'll see in a separate video. But I'm just putting this in here. So the reframe, you start by, this is the difference, you compare in contrast, you share your story. And then you end this reframe section by working to create hope and anticipation, like asking a series of questions that all have yes answers, can you see how this changes everything? Can you see how much easier this is in the old way? Are you ready to learn how to implement this? And they're saying yes, yes, yes. And then you drop right into your content section after that.
